digest.top_line: AREST: adjuvant RT lifts 3-yr LRFS 89.2% vs 80.9% (HR 0.52) in intermediate-risk pT1-2N0 OSCC, first phase III here; no OS/DFS gain.
digest.tldr: Head-neck carries the day: AREST is the first phase III proving adjuvant RT cuts local-regional failure in intermediate-risk early OSCC (HR 0.52), though the LRC gain doesn't reach OS/DFS. Prostate offers a delivery-gap counterpoint: only 67% of high-risk RT pts get guideline-concordant ADT, with site of care an independent driver.

sites.intro: Statewide quality-consortium look at how often high-risk RT pts actually get guideline-concordant ADT.

studies.name: MROQC ADT Practice Patterns
studies.tldr: Only 67% of 553 high-risk RT pts got guideline-concordant ADT (≥18mo); ARPI intensification reached 23.2% of STAMPEDE-eligible pts post-publication.
details: 📊 1° outcome: intended guideline-concordant ADT (≥18mo) recommended in 67.0% of 553 pts; any ADT in 91.3%
🔍 Prospective MROQC statewide quality-consortium data, 26 centers, June 2020-Nov 2024; intact high-risk M0/N0-1 prostate
📊 ARPI intensification among the 27.9% meeting STAMPEDE M0 criteria rose 0% → 23.2% pre vs post STAMPEDE M0 publication
⚠️ Facility-level variability persisted on MVA (P<.0001): site of care independently drives ADT recommendation
⚠️ Intended ADT recommendation captured, not delivered or completed ADT
⚠️ Single-state consortium; no clinical outcome or efficacy endpoint
🔗 Reference standards: 2022 AUA/ASTRO (18-36mo ADT with definitive RT); STAMPEDE M0 (ARPI for ≥2 of cT3/4, GG4-5, PSA ≥40, cN1)

verdict.rationale: Descriptive prospective practice audit, not an efficacy trial; no clinical outcome or randomized comparison, so it documents a guideline-adherence gap rather than changing standard of care.
verdict.audience: High-risk M0/N0-1 prostate cancer, definitive RT candidates

studies.significance: Two decision-relevant gaps: among the 27.9% meeting STAMPEDE M0 criteria, ARPI intensification reached only 23.2% even post-publication, and facility remained an independent predictor of guideline-concordant ADT (P<.0001) after adjusting for GG, cN1, and PSA. Site of care, not just tumor biology, is setting ADT intensity for high-risk RT pts.
studies.monday_clinic: For a high-risk RT candidate meeting STAMPEDE M0 criteria (≥2 of cT3/4, GG4-5, PSA ≥40, cN1), this flags that guideline-recommended ARPI intensification is often omitted in practice; it does not apply to intermediate-risk disease or establish that intensification changes outcomes.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Prospective practice-pattern study** within MROQC, a statewide radiation-oncology quality consortium. **553 pts across 26 centers**, June 2020 to Nov 2024. Captures **intended** ADT recommendations, not delivered treatment or outcomes.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Intact, **high-risk M0/N0-1** prostate cancer planned for definitive RT. **GG 4-5 in 75.0%**, cN1 19.9%, cT3/4 13.3%, PSA ≥20 in 40.0%.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary: intended guideline-concordant ADT (≥18 mo).** Secondary: ARPI adoption before vs after STAMPEDE M0; facility-level variability via mixed-effects model.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Any ADT recommended in **91.3%**; guideline-concordant in **67.0%**. ARPI among STAMPEDE-eligible (27.9% of cohort) rose **0% → 23.2%** post-publication. **Facility was an independent predictor (P<.0001).**

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: **2022 AUA/ASTRO** recommends 18-36 mo ADT with definitive RT; **STAMPEDE M0** supports ARPI intensification for ≥2 of cT3/4, GG4-5, PSA ≥40, or cN1. This audit shows both under-applied in practice.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents high-risk M0/N0-1 prostate cancer treated with definitive RT in a single US state. Does not represent intermediate/low-risk disease, surgically managed pts, or delivered (vs intended) ADT outcomes.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Intended, not delivered** ADT; single-state consortium limits generalizability; **no clinical outcome or efficacy endpoint**; ARPI adoption window short after STAMPEDE M0 publication.

open_questions: Drivers of persistent facility-level variation in ADT duration
Whether intended ADT recommendations match delivered treatment
How to raise ARPI intensification in STAMPEDE-eligible pts

sites.intro: First randomised evidence for adjuvant RT in intermediate-risk early oral-cavity cancer.

studies.name: AREST
studies.tldr: 3-yr LRFS 89.2% vs 80.9%, HR 0.52 (0.30-0.91), p=0.02 for adjuvant RT in intermediate-risk pT1-2N0 OSCC; no OS/DFS benefit.

details.text: 📊 Adjuvant RT vs observation across endpoints

details: 🔍 Phase III open-label RCT, N=392 (191 RT vs 201 obs), multicenter India
🔍 RT: 60Gy in 30 fractions over 6 wks to resected tumor-bed + at-risk neck nodal region

subdetails: Adequate surgery: margins ≥5mm + ipsilateral level I-III neck dissection ≥16 nodes
Intermediate-risk = DOI ≥5-≤10mm, PNI, LVE, or poor differentiation

details: 🔗 First phase III RCT here; prior intermediate-risk early-OSCC RT guidance was retrospective-only
⚠️ LRC gain did not translate to DFS/OS benefit at median 47.2-mo f/u
⚠️ Oral-tongue subgroup benefited more than buccal mucosa; exploratory, hypothesis-generating
📐 Powered for HR 0.626 assuming 70% 3-yr observation LRFS; actual observation arm 80.9%

verdict.rationale: First phase 3 RCT for intermediate-risk early OSCC, primary LRFS endpoint hit, but backs retrospective-era practice and shows no survival benefit; conference abstract.
verdict.audience: pT1-2 pN0 oral SCC, adequately resected, ≥1 intermediate-risk feature

studies.significance: Level-1 evidence where only retrospective data existed: adjuvant RT (60Gy/30fx) cut 3-yr locoregional recurrence in adequately-resected pT1-2N0 OSCC (LRFS 89.2 vs 80.9, HR 0.52), with benefit concentrated in oral tongue over buccal mucosa. Moves the offer-RT-vs-observe decision for intermediate-risk features; no OS/DFS gain yet.
studies.monday_clinic: In adequately-resected pT1-2 pN0 oral SCC with intermediate-risk features, especially oral tongue, this supports the case for adjuvant RT to reduce locoregional recurrence; it does not establish a survival benefit and the buccal-mucosa signal was weaker.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Multicenter **phase 3 open-label RCT** from India, 1:1, **N=392** (191 adjuvant RT vs 201 observation). Stratified by oral-cavity subsite, PNI/LVE, and differentiation. Median follow-up 47.2 months.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: pT1-2 pN0 OSCC after **adequate surgery** (margins ≥5mm, ipsilateral level I-III neck dissection ≥16 nodes) with ≥1 intermediate-risk feature: DOI ≥5-≤10mm, PNI, LVE, or poor differentiation.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: **60Gy in 30 fractions** over 6 weeks to the resected tumor-bed and at-risk neck nodal region.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary:** loco-regional recurrence-free survival (**LRFS**), 3-yr KM estimate. Secondary: DFS, OS.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: LRFS favored adjuvant RT on ITT and per-protocol; DFS and OS not significantly different.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: First phase 3 RCT to test adjuvant RT specifically for intermediate-risk, node-negative early OSCC; prior support for this practice was retrospective.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents adequately-resected pT1-2 pN0 oral SCC with an intermediate-risk feature. Does not represent node-positive, positive-margin, or high-risk disease, where postoperative RT is already standard.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Open-label conference abstract; **no DFS/OS benefit** at median 47.2 mo. Observation LRFS (80.9%) exceeded the 70% powering assumption. Oral-tongue subgroup benefit exploratory.

open_questions: Does locoregional control translate to a survival benefit with longer follow-up?
Can adjuvant RT be safely omitted in lower-risk intermediate subsets?

related_trials.brief_title: Impact of Postoperative Radiotherapy Versus PD-1 Inhibitor Maintenance on Survival in Resectable Locally Advanced Oral and Oropharyngeal Squamous Cell Carcinoma
related_trials.brief_summary:
Neoadjuvant immunochemotherapy (NAIC) has demonstrated promising pathological and survival outcomes in patients with resectable locally advanced oral and oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (LA-OSCC/OPSCC). However, the optimal postoperative management strategy following NAIC and radical surgery remains undefined, particularly regarding the necessity of postoperative radiotherapy and the potential role of PD-1 inhibitor maintenance therapy.

This single-center, ambispective cohort study aims…

Locally Advanced Oropharyngeal Squamous Cell Carcinoma

papers.title: Androgen Deprivation Therapy Practice Patterns in High-Risk Prostate Cancer Treated With Definitive Radiotherapy: Prospective Results From a Statewide Quality Consortium
papers.abstract: PURPOSE The 2022 AUA/ASTRO guidelines recommend 18-36 months of androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) with definitive radiotherapy for localized, high-risk prostate cancer. The STAMPEDE M0 trial supports intensification with androgen receptor pathway inhibitors (ARPIs) for patients with ≥2 cT3/T4, Grade Group [GG] 4-5, prostate-specific antigen (PSA) ≥40 ng/mL, or cN1. Given advances in imaging, risk stratification, and treatment delivery, we characterized contemporary practice patterns using prospective data from the Michigan Radiation Oncology Quality Consortium (MROQC). METHODS Patients enrolled in MROQC with intact, high-risk M0/N0-1 prostate cancer were included. Clinical information, including intended ADT duration and ARPI use, was prospectively collected. The primary outcome was intended guideline-concordant ADT (GC-ADT, ≥18 months). Multivariable analyses (MVA) assessed associations between clinical factors and GC-ADT recommendations. We compared the adoption of ARPI with standard therapies before and after the publication of STAMPEDE M0. Facility-level variability was evaluated using a mixed-effects model, with the treatment site as a random intercept. RESULTS Between June 2020 and November 2024, 553 patients across 26 centers were included: cT3/4 (13.3%), cN1 (19.9%), GG 4-5 (75.0%), and PSA ≥20 ng/mL (40.0%). Overall, 91.3% were recommended ADT, with 67.0% being guideline-concordant. On MVA, GC-ADT was significantly associated with cN1 (odds ratio [OR], 2.94 [95% CI, 1.44 to 5.99]), GG (GG4 OR, 6.23 [95% CI, 2.85 to 13.62]; GG5 OR, 9.45 [95% CI, 4.46 to 20.06]), and PSA ≥40 (OR, 3.64 [95% CI, 1.22–10.87]). Facility-level variability persisted in the MVA ( P < .0001). Among the 27.9% who met meeting STAMPEDE criteria, ARPI recommendations increased from 0% prepublication to 23.2% afterward. CONCLUSION Within a statewide quality consortium, guideline-concordant ADT recommendations occurred in two thirds of patients, with ARPI intensification in under 25% among STAMPEDE-eligible patients. These findings highlight the need for individualized ADT strategies and collaborative efforts to standardize high-quality care.

papers.title: Adjuvant radiotherapy versus observation following curative surgery for early-stage oral squamous cell carcinoma (AREST; CTRI/2017/07/009114).
authors: AREST Study - Head and Neck Cooperative Oncology Group
papers.abstract: 6000 Background: The role of adjuvant radiotherapy (RT) in early-stage, node-negative oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) with one or more intermediate risk factors - such as depth of invasion (DOI) ≥5 to ≤10mm, perineural invasion (PNI), lymphovascular emboli (LVE), or poor differentiation - remains debatable and is largely based on retrospective data. This multicenter, open-label, phase III randomized controlled trial was designed to assess the impact of post-operative adjuvant RT in this setting. Methods: Patients with early-stage (pT1-T2), node-negative (pN0) OSCC undergoing adequate surgery (defined as clear margins ≥5mm and at least ipsilateral level I-III neck dissection with ≥16 nodes) with presence of one or more intermediate risk factors were screened. Eligible patients underwent stratified randomization (oral cavity subsite, PNI/LVE, and differentiation) in 1:1 ratio to either observation or adjuvant RT (60Gy in 30 fractions over 6-weeks) to the resected tumor-bed and at-risk neck nodal region after written informed consent. Primary endpoint was loco-regional recurrence-free survival (LRFS) measured from randomization to first documented event of local and/or regional recurrence from index cancer. All time-to-event outcomes were computed using Kaplan-Meier (KM) method with log-rank test for comparison and expressed as 3-year point estimates with 95% confidence intervals (CI). The planned sample size (N=392) provided 80% power at an α of 0.05 to detect a Hazard Ratio (HR) of 0.6256, assuming 3-year LRFS of 70% in the observation arm. Results: Following curative surgery, a total of 392 patients were randomized (191 to adjuvant RT; 201 to observation). Baseline characteristics were balanced between the two arms. At a median follow-up of 47.2 months (inter-quartile range=30-59.4 months), 3-year KM estimate of LRFS was 89.2% in adjuvant RT arm vs 80.9% in observation arm (HR=0.52, 95%CI=0.30-0.91; p=0.02) in the intention-to-treat (ITT) population and 91.1% vs 80.9% (HR=0.43, 95%CI=0.23-0.80; p=0.01) on per-protocol (PP) analyses. Cumulative incidence of loco-regional failure with death as competing event was 10.6% (95%CI=6.1%-15.1%) with adjuvant RT and 18.9% (95%CI=13.3%-24.6%) with observation (HR=0.52, 95%CI=0.30-0.91; p=0.021) in the ITT population and 8.7% (95%CI=4.3%-13.1%) vs 18.9% (95%CI=13.3%-24.6%) (HR=0.43, 95%CI=0.23-0.79; p=0.007) on PP analyses. Disease-free and overall survival were not significantly different between the two arms. Subgroup analysis identified oral tongue deriving higher benefit of adjuvant RT compared to buccal mucosa. Conclusions: Adjuvant RT significantly reduces risk of loco-regional recurrence for early-stage, node-negative adequately resected OSCC, particularly oral tongue. However, such reduction in loco-regional failure does not translate into significant survival benefit. Clinical trial information: CTRI/2017/07/009114.
